mnemonics.title: R.W.A. Remember File Modes
mnemonics.content: R = Read (default, fails if file doesn't exist), W = Write (creates/overwrites), A = Append (creates/adds to end). Think: 'Read Wins When Absent' - R fails if absent, W creates if absent.

mnemonics.title: PAUSE Before Closing
mnemonics.content: P = Position the pointer (seek), A = Act (read/write), U = Use (tell to check position), S = Save (flush), E = Exit (close). Always close or flush to save data!

mnemonics.title: WORA - Write Once, Read All
mnemonics.content: W = Write mode (overwrites), O = Open, R = Read mode, A = Append mode. Use 'W' to create fresh files, 'A' to add more later without losing old data.

mnemonics.title: JSON - Java's Script Object Notation
mnemonics.content: Remember: JSON stores data like a Python dictionary - keys in quotes, values can be strings, numbers, lists, or nested objects. Use json.load() and json.dump().

mnemonics.title: CSV - Comma Separates Values
mnemonics.content: Think of CSV like a spreadsheet: rows are lines, columns are separated by commas. Use csv.reader() for reading and csv.writer() for writing.

mnemonics.title: With Great Power Comes Clean Code
mnemonics.content: WITH statement automatically closes files: 'with open('file.txt', 'r') as f:' - the file closes even if errors occur. No more 'f.close()' needed!

mnemonics.title: OS - Operating System helper
mnemonics.content: OS module is your file manager: os.remove() to delete, os.rename() to rename, os.listdir() to list files, os.path.exists() to check if file is there.

mnemonics.title: PATHLIB - The Modern Path Handler
mnemonics.content: Path('file.txt').exists() - pathlib uses object-oriented paths. Path objects have methods like .is_file(), .is_dir(), .suffix, .stem. Think: 'Path is Path!'

acronyms.expansion: opens a file and returns a file object
acronyms.usage: Basic syntax: open(filename, mode). Always use with 'with' statement or remember to close() manually.
Used for structured data storage. json.dump(obj, file) writes Python dict to JSON file. json.load(file) reads JSON into Python dict.
Used for tabular data. csv.writer() creates rows, csv.reader() iterates through lines. Each row is a list of strings.
File management: os.getcwd(), os.chdir(), os.remove(), os.rename(), os.mkdir(), os.rmdir().
Returns integer (bytes from start). Use with seek() to move around: seek(0) goes to start, seek(0,2) goes to end.

acronyms.expansion: seeks to a specific position in file

acronyms.usage: Modern alternative to os.path. Path('folder/file.txt') creates path object. Methods: .exists(), .is_file(), .read_text(), .write_text().
shutil.copy() to copy file content, shutil.move() to move, shutil.rmtree() to delete folders. Think 'shell utility' for complex operations.

visualTips.concept: File Pointer as a Tape Head
visualTips.visualization: Imagine a cassette tape head. When you read, it moves forward. tell() shows where the head is, seek() moves the head back. Reading consumes position like playing a tape.
Read (r) = Blue pen - can only read, can't change. Write (w) = Red pen - scribbles over everything, starts fresh. Append (a) = Green pen - adds notes at the end without erasing.
Picture automatic sliding doors at a mall. 'with' opens the door (file), lets you do business (read/write), and closes automatically when done - no matter what!
Text mode (t) = Human readable letters. Binary (b) = Raw computer bytes (images, videos). Think: 'b' for bytes, 't' for text. Use 'rb' or 'wb' for images/executables.
JSON data is like Russian nested dolls (matryoshka). A dict can contain lists, which contain more dicts. json.load() unpacks these boxes into Python objects.

visualTips.concept: Pathlib Path Object as a Address
visualTips.visualization: Think of Path('C:/Users/John/doc.txt') like a postal address. It has parts (segments), a parent (folder), a name (filename), and suffix (.txt). You can check if it exists like verifying an address.

analogies.analogy: Like borrowing a book from a library. 'r' means just reading (library reading room - can't mark), 'w' means you get a fresh notebook to write new notes, 'a' means adding your notes to the margin of an existing book.
readline() is like reading one page at a time manually. readlines() is like scanning the entire book and creating a list of all pages at once - faster but uses more memory for big books.
write() is like writing one sentence by hand. writelines() is like feeding multiple sentences to a printer at once - more efficient for many lines, but doesn't add newlines automatically.

Think of buffering like a hotel checkout desk. When you write, data goes to a buffer (desk) first, then gets 'flushed' to the actual file (stored) later. flush() forces immediate writing like paying immediately.

os.path is like old-fashioned directions: 'Go left at the corner, then right.' Pathlib is like a GPS with objects: 'Navigate to the Address object, then check if it exists.' More modern and intuitive.
File operations are like driving - always expect the unexpected! FileNotFoundError = wrong address. PermissionError = locked door. Use try-except-finally or 'with' to handle these gracefully.
Each CSV line is like a row in Excel. The commas are the column dividers. csv.reader gives you each row as a list: ['Name', 'Age', 'City'] - easy to access by index like row[0].
shutil.copy is like photocopying a document (creates new file with content). os.rename is like putting a new label on the same folder - faster but risky if moving across drives.
